text:
CT ABDOMEN AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
SURVEILLANCE STUDY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 67-year-old female with history of pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma of the pancreatic head, status post neoadjuvant FOLFIRINOX followed by chemoradiation with capecitabine, and pylorus-preserving pancreaticoduodenectomy with SMV segmental resection and reconstruction (ypT3N1M0, R0 resection). Subsequently completed adjuvant gemcitabine and capecitabine. Now approximately 17 months post-surgery. Presenting for routine surveillance. CA 19-9 reportedly elevated on recent laboratory draw. Evaluate for locoregional recurrence or distant metastatic disease.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the abdomen and pelvis was performed with intravenous contrast (iohexol 350 mgI/mL, 120 mL) administered via antecubital vein at a rate of 4 mL/sec. Imaging was acquired in the portal venous phase following a 70-second delay and in a delayed phase at 180 seconds. Sagittal and coronal reformations were generated from the axial dataset. Slice thickness: 2.5 mm axial, 3 mm coronal and sagittal reformations. Comparison is made to prior surveillance CT of the abdomen and pelvis performed approximately 4 months earlier, which demonstrated no evidence of recurrent disease.

FINDINGS:

LIVER AND BILIARY TRACT:
The liver is normal in size and morphology. A new focal hepatic lesion is identified in segment VI, measuring 1.4 cm in greatest axial dimension (not present on comparison study from 4 months prior). This lesion is hypodense relative to the hepatic parenchyma on portal venous phase images and demonstrates a thin, somewhat irregular peripheral enhancement pattern without the progressive fill-in pattern that would be expected for a hemangioma. On delayed-phase images, the lesion does not show the expected centripetal enhancement of a benign vascular lesion, instead appearing persistently hypodense centrally with subtle residual peripheral blush. There is no macroscopic internal fat or calcification within the lesion. The lesion has a well-circumscribed but non-encapsulated morphology. No satellite lesions are identified in the adjacent hepatic parenchyma. No other hepatic lesions are identified in segments I through VIII. The intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated. The gallbladder is surgically absent (cholecystectomy as part of prior Whipple procedure). The biliary anastomosis at the hepaticojejunostomy site is not clearly delineated but there is no proximal biliary ductal dilation to suggest anastomotic stricture. No pneumobilia. The extrahepatic biliary system appears unremarkable in the context of the prior surgical reconstruction.

PANCREAS AND SURGICAL BED:
The pancreatic remnant (body and tail) is identified in the expected location. There is no focal mass lesion within the remnant pancreas. A small residual pancreatic duct is visible in the body, measuring up to 3 mm, which is unchanged from the prior study. The expected post-Whipple fibrotic changes are present in the surgical bed of the pancreatic head and uncinate region. No discrete soft-tissue mass is identified in the surgical bed or in the peripancreatic region. No suspicious adenopathy is identified in the peripancreatic or periceliac distribution. The previously placed biliary metallic stent has been surgically removed (Whipple specimen). The pancreaticojejunostomy anastomosis is not separately visible but there is no upstream ductal dilation to suggest anastomotic failure.

VASCULAR STRUCTURES:
The superior mesenteric artery demonstrates normal caliber and opacification. The superior mesenteric vein is patent with normal flow void at the site of the prior vascular reconstruction and end-to-end anastomosis, now appearing as a mildly irregular but unobstructed venous segment; this is unchanged from the prior study. The portal vein, splenic vein, and hepatic veins are patent. The celiac axis and its branches, including the common hepatic artery and splenic artery, are unremarkable.

SPLEEN:
The spleen is normal in size (craniocaudal span approximately 9.8 cm). No focal splenic lesions.

ADRENAL GLANDS:
Bilateral adrenal glands appear normal in size and morphology without discrete nodularity.

KIDNEYS AND URETERS:
The kidneys are normal in size with symmetric cortical thickness bilaterally. No hydronephrosis. No renal mass or stone. Simple-appearing right renal cortical cyst, 1.1 cm, stable.

GASTROINTESTINAL TRACT:
The gastric remnant (pylorus-preserving reconstruction) is not distended but appears intact without wall thickening or perilesional inflammatory change. The hepaticojejunostomy and duodenojejunostomy limbs of the Roux reconstruction are visible in the right upper quadrant without obstruction, dilation, or wall thickening. The remaining small bowel loops are normal in caliber and wall thickness. The colon is unremarkable. No bowel obstruction.

PERITONEUM AND RETROPERITONEUM:
No ascites is present. No peritoneal nodularity or thickening to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis. The retroperitoneum is without bulky lymphadenopathy; scattered small para-aortic lymph nodes are present, the largest measuring 7 mm in short axis, within the range of normal. No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No pelvic free fluid.

LUNGS (VISUALIZED LUNG BASES):
The visualized lung bases are clear. No pleural effusion. No pulmonary nodule is visible at the lung bases.

BONY STRUCTURES:
No destructive or suspicious osseous lesion is identified in the imaged skeleton. Multilevel degenerative changes of the lumbar spine are noted, consistent with patient age. No pathologic fracture.

OTHER:
Postoperative changes are noted in the midline anterior abdominal wall consistent with the prior open surgical approach. Small ventral hernia at the superior aspect of the midline incision, measuring approximately 2.2 cm in width, without herniation of bowel or evidence of incarceration; this appears similar in size to the prior study.

IMPRESSION:

1. NEW 1.4 CM HYPODENSE HEPATIC LESION IN SEGMENT VI with an atypical peripheral enhancement pattern that does not conform to benign vascular or cystic etiology. Given the clinical context of a patient with recently treated pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, this lesion must be regarded as highly suspicious for hepatic metastasis. This lesion was not present on comparison imaging from 4 months prior, underscoring its recent development. Urgent further characterization with dedicated hepatobiliary MRI is strongly warranted.

2. No evidence of locoregional recurrence in the pancreatic surgical bed or mesenteric root.

3. Patent SMV reconstruction at the prior vascular anastomosis site, unchanged.

4. No peritoneal deposits, no suspicious adenopathy, no other distant site of concern on this examination.

5. Small stable ventral hernia at the midline laparotomy site without complication.

Correlate with serum CA 19-9 and CEA tumor markers. Clinical and multidisciplinary follow-up recommended on an urgent basis given the high clinical concern for hepatic metastatic disease in this patient.
